document.title: M1 Lattice: Philosophy and Direction
document.purpose: Conceptual foundation of the M1 Lattice system, describing entities, links, aspects, and tables as a distributed identity-centered data model.

core_idea: The M1 Lattice is a non-authoritative, identity-merged distributed graph. Entities exist independently, and structure emerges through the merging of contributions across systems via shared identity. Entities may be given simple, foundational information, such as descriptions, URLs, tags, or images, to make them generically browsable. They can then be enriched over time through the addition of aspects, each contributing a distinct dimension of meaning. Entities may also be connected through links, which themselves can be treated as entities within the lattice. Meaning emerges and evolves within this structure, beginning informally and becoming more precise over time, forming a gradient from human-readable hints to formal, referential definitions.

entities.description: An entity is a uniquely identifiable unit of existence within the system.
entities.identity: Defined by a stable primary identifier (such as a GUID), with optional human-meaningful identifiers (such as tag URIs) that may also refer to the entity.
entities.properties: All other properties are optional and attachable, not intrinsic.

links.description: Links are entities that express relationships between other entities.
properties: may include human-readable type hints and formal type references
links.purpose: Allow relationships to be represented, extended, and referenced independently of the entities they connect.

aspects.description: Aspects describe independent dimensions or perspectives of an entity.
properties: enable participation in multiple conceptual systems

tables.description: Mappings between identifiers and resource locations.
purpose: resolve entity IDs to file paths
bridge abstract identity and concrete storage

lattice_nature.description: The system forms a lattice rather than a hierarchy.
properties: partial ordering instead of strict hierarchy

design_principles: Identity is stable; structure is fluid.
Links exist independently of the entities they connect.
Aspects describe independent dimensions or perspectives of an entity.
Data formats do not matter; they can always be translated.
Meaning evolves along a gradient, from simple identities, to richly defined entities with many aspects, and detailed links to other entities.
The lattice represents perspectives and claims, not enforced truths.
vision: A distributed data universe where any object can be referenced universally, systems interoperate through shared identity, and structure emerges from use rather than prescription.
